lines.text: Mr. Okafor stands at the front of the room with a stack of papers, like it's already given him a headache.
Alright. Settle. This one's actually worth listening to.
lines.action: taps two fingers on the desk

lines.text: Semester project. Twenty percent of your grade. Presentation in front of the whole grade, plus a panel, in three weeks.
Three weeks? Easy.
lines.action: leans his chair back on two legs

lines.text: That's fifteen school days. Fourteen if you don't count the pep rally, which you shouldn't, because nothing gets done that day.
...See, this is why I like being near Priya.
Groups are groups. Nobody gets to pick their favorite people — that's not what this is for.
Table four. Dez Bellamy. Priya Chen. Mo Alvarez. Charlie Osei.
Okay. That's... a combination.
Across the room, Mo looks up. Just for a second. Like he's checking whether she remembers.
...Great.
Alright, table four, let's go — teamwork makes the dream work, I don't make the rules.
You say that like it's not the single most annoying sentence in the English language.
It's a classic.
This is gonna be interesting.
Library study room. Four chairs, one whiteboard, and a very optimistic amount of silence.
Okay, so — hot take. I don't think we need to start from zero.
We haven't started at all yet, so that's not really a hot take, that's just—
My sister did basically this exact project last year. Same rubric and everything. I'm, like, ninety percent sure I still have the files.
...Ninety percent.
We just tweak some words. Swap a few slides around. Boom, done, and we're not spending our whole week in here.
I mean — if it's just for structure, that's not the worst— I don't know. I don't love it.
It's not that deep, Priya.
What do you think? You're quiet over there.
Everyone looks at Charlie. Mo included, though he'd never admit he's waiting for anything.
Dez blinks, clearly not expecting that answer.
It's not that deep, man. Nobody's gonna know.
...I mean, she's kind of right, though. If we get caught it's not just a bad grade, it's a whole meeting-with-parents thing.
Okay, okay. Fine. We do it ourselves. Geez.
...Cool.
lines.action: glances at player, something shifting in his posture

lines.text: Okay. Good. I can work with 'own work.' I like 'own work.'
See, panic was step four. We didn't even need it.
Dez grins like he already won this conversation before it started.
See? Told you it's not that deep.
I'll text my sister, tell her thanks in advance.
...Okay.

lines.action: something in his face closes off, looks down at his own notebook

lines.text: Relax, everybody. This is gonna be the easiest twenty percent of your life.
It's fine. It's just a starting point. ...Right?
...Fine. Whatever's faster.
Okay — that actually works. We use her outline for structure and formatting, but every fact, every source, is ours.
...That's the first thing today that made sense.
Wow. High praise from the guy who's said four words.
I'm rationing them.
Okay. Maybe this group's gonna be fine.
Priya's kitchen table, days later. Papers everywhere. Dez's phone is very interesting to him right now.
No, that's not — MLA doesn't work like that, why does it keep—
You good?
lines.action: not looking up from his phone

lines.text: No, but that's not new information.
Need a hand?
I need you to invent a ninth day of the week, but sure. You can check the works cited page.
Mo's been quiet for a while, doodling in the margin of his notes. Small, careful robot shapes.
...Hey. Uh.
I don't really get this part. The whole second half of my section.
Wait, since when?
Since the beginning. I've just been writing stuff down that sounds right.
Mo.
I mean — it's fine. I'll figure it out.
He will not, in fact, figure it out on his own.
Charlie pulls her chair over, notebook between them.
Okay, walk me through what you do get. We'll work backwards from there.
I mean — I get the first part. It's the cause-and-effect part that loses me. Like why B happens because of A instead of just... also happening.
That's actually a really specific thing to get stuck on. That's good — means you're close.
That supposed to make me feel better?
Kind of, yeah. Is it working?
...A little.
They go slow. No shortcuts, no rushing. Somewhere around the third example, something clicks.
Oh — oh wait. So it's not that A causes B, it's that A causes the thing that causes B.
Yes! Okay, see, you got it.
Huh. Look at that.
...You draw robots too?
That's — yeah. That's a me thing.
Fair enough. Just checking.
Just copy the summary from the textbook. Change a couple words so it's not identical. Nobody's gonna quiz you line by line.
...Okay.
Mo copies it down, word by word, careful and small.
Yeah. This is fine.
...You want me to look over it after?
Sure. Whatever.
You should probably just ask Mr. Okafor tomorrow before class. He'll explain it better than I can.
...Yeah. Probably.
I mean — I don't love the idea of, like, announcing I'm behind. But sure.
It's not announcing. It's just asking a question.
Yeah. No, I know.
lines.action: goes back to his notebook, doesn't bring it up again

lines.text: ...He's not gonna ask.
What?
Nothing. Never mind.
Group chat, 9:47 PM. Priya sends Dez's slides for review.
dez sent his slides finally, can everyone look before tmrw
Charlie scrolls through. Something's off — the phrasing is too smooth, too clean, in a way none of Dez's texts ever are.
Wait. I've read this sentence before. Word for word.
dez did you copy this from somewhere
??? no. I mean — I looked stuff up, that's not the same thing
charlie what do you mean
like literally paste this sentence into a search bar. it's word for word from a site.
A long pause. The typing bubble appears. Disappears. Appears again.
okay maybe I didn't change enough of it. it's basically the same info, who cares
we're not gonna get caught. it's a middle school project not the news
that's not really the point though
Just three dots. No words. Somehow that says the most.
Video call, past 10 PM. Everyone looks tired. Dez looks cornered.
I'm not trying to get you in trouble. I just — we can't turn this in like this. It's not fair to the rest of us either.
It's fine. I'll just... reword it more.
That's not really fixing it, though. You still don't actually know the material if it's just reworded.
...Yeah. I know.
I just — I didn't get it as fast as everyone else. Figured it was easier to find something that already sounded smart.
You could've just said that.
Yeah, well. Kind of hard to say the smart group thinks you're not smart enough.
...Nobody thinks that.
It's the most Mo's said all week. Dez doesn't quite know what to do with it.
Okay. So let's just — go through it. Together. Right now. It's ugly, but it's ours.
...Fine. Let's just get it over with.
It takes two more hours. Nobody complains, exactly. By the end, the slides are messier, less polished — and completely theirs.
I hate that I'm gonna remember this part of the project more than any other part.
...Thanks. For not just, like, telling everyone.
Wasn't gonna.
Hallway, before first bell. Mr. Okafor's unlocking his classroom door.
Mr. Okafor? Can I talk to you for a sec?
Charlie Osei. That's a full-name kind of walk-up. What's going on?
lines.action: turns, key still in the lock

lines.text: It's about the group project. Dez's section — some of it's copied. Word-for-word from a website. We just found out last night.
I appreciate you telling me. That's not nothing, coming here before class.
lines.action: taps two fingers against the door

lines.text: I'm not trying to get him in trouble. I just — we need time to actually fix it right, and there's not much time left.
Anyone else know you're telling me this?
No. Just me.
Okay. I'll give the group an extra evening. And I'll have a word with Dez — not about you. About the work.
Okay. Thank you.
Nice recovery, by the way. Most groups just would've bluffed and hoped nobody noticed. You noticed the difference. Good.
We'll see if Dez agrees with you.
That's not really the part you can control.
11:58 PM. The chat's gone quiet.
so are we just... doing this? like this?
No response from Charlie. The message just sits there, the little 'delivered' under it feeling louder than it should.
ok
Mo doesn't respond at all. Not even three dots this time.
It's late. Everyone's tired. Maybe it'll be fine. Maybe.
The slides stay exactly as they are.
The auditorium. Whole grade, plus a panel of teachers at a folding table up front. Priya's practically vibrating; Dez looks weirdly calm for once.
Okay, remember, we breathe between slides, we do not talk over each other, we—
Priya. We got this.
Don't — don't say it'll be fine.
The presentation goes smoothly, slide after slide. Then Dez's section comes up.
Quick follow-up on that middle slide — can you walk us through why that cause leads to that effect? Not just what happens, but why.
lines.action: leans forward from the panel table

lines.text: Dez freezes for half a second. Just half a second. But it's there.
Right, so, basically — it's kind of a direct... result. Of the, uh—
He trails off. The panel waits. Priya's pen has stopped tapping entirely.
So, honestly — this was the part that gave us the most trouble as a group. We understand that A sets off a chain, but the exact mechanism between A and B, we're still not a hundred percent solid on.
Based on what we did find, it's probably related to the timing difference — but we didn't have a source solid enough to say it for sure, so we didn't want to present it like we did.
The panel exchanges a look. One of them actually smiles.
That's a more honest answer than half the projects I've seen today. Good.
...Yeah. What she said.
It's not a smooth save. But it's a real one, and somehow that lands better.
Okay. Okay, that was — good. That was good.
—so, structurally, it's basically the backbone of the whole argument, which is why—
Mm. Interesting. Thank you.
The moment passes. The presentation moves on. But something about it doesn't sit right.
After, Priya won't quite meet Charlie's eyes. Mo stares at the floor, hands in his pockets.
We didn't get caught. Why doesn't that feel like winning?
Grades posted. The group did fine — 'fine' meaning something different depending on how the last three weeks went.
Mo catches up to Charlie at her locker, hands shoved in his pockets.
Hey. Uh — got a sec?
Yeah, of course.
I mean — I don't really know how to say this without it sounding weird. So I'm just gonna say it weird.
That's usually how I do it too.
Hallway, after the last bell. Lockers slamming, the ordinary noise of everyone else's day.
It was messy. Actually messy, more than once. People almost took the easy way out. I almost did too.
But you didn't.
Not the whole way, no.
Yeah. I noticed.
Okay, I'm adding you to the group chat for next semester's project. Before you even ask. Don't read too much into it.
Wouldn't dream of it.
Hallway, after the last bell. The kind of quiet that only happens right after everyone else has gone home.
It wasn't perfect. There's stuff I'd do differently if we did it again.
Like what?
I don't know. Said things sooner, maybe. Instead of waiting until it was already a problem.
At least you're saying it.
That's more self-awareness than most eighth graders manage in a whole year, for the record.
I'll take it.
Hallway, after the last bell. Charlie keeps her voice light, easy — the way she always does when she'd rather not.
Grade turned out fine, though. That's the main thing, right?
...Yeah. Sure.
He starts to walk off, then stops, like he almost said something else. He doesn't.
He wanted to talk about it. Actually talk. And I changed the subject.

options.text: "I mean... I think we should just do our own thing. Actually write it ourselves."
"Whatever's faster, I guess. It's not that deep."
"What if we just use hers for structure, but do the actual research ourselves?"

a.text: I mean... I think we should just do our own thing. Actually write it ourselves.

b.text: See, this is why I like this group.

c.text: What if we just use hers for, like, the structure? But we actually do the research part ourselves.

options.text: "Okay, no — show me what you've got. We'll go through it together."
"Just copy the summary from the textbook, tweak a few words. Easier for everyone."
"You should just ask Mr. Okafor tomorrow. He'll explain it better than us."

a.text: Okay, no — show me what you've got. We'll go through it together.

b.text: Honestly? Just copy the summary from the textbook, tweak a couple words. Nobody's gonna dig that deep.

b.action: writes it down, doesn't look up

c.text: You should probably just ask Mr. Okafor tomorrow. He'll explain it better than any of us.

options.text: "I'm gonna talk to Okafor before class — not to get you in trouble, just so we have time to fix it."
"It's late. Let's just... deal with it tomorrow."

b.text: I'm gonna talk to Okafor before class. not to get you in trouble, just — we need more time to fix it right.

c.text: it's late. let's just... deal with it tomorrow
The message sits there, unanswered. Mo doesn't respond at all.

options.text: "Actually, can I jump in? That part gave our whole group trouble — I can walk through what we do understand."
Say nothing. Let Dez keep talking.

a.text: Actually, can I jump in? That part gave our whole group some trouble — I can walk through what we do understand, and where we got stuck.

b.text: —so it's basically a direct cause, like a one-to-one thing, and that's why it's significant, structurally speaking—
A teacher's eyebrow lifts. Just slightly.

options.text: "There were, like, three points where this could've gone really wrong. Doing it right actually mattered."
"We got a good grade, right? That's what matters."
"It wasn't perfect. There's stuff I'd do differently."

a.text: Honestly? There were, like, three different points where this could've gone really wrong. And it mattered a lot that it didn't — not just for the grade.

b.text: I mean, we got a good grade, right? That's what matters.

c.text: It wasn't perfect. There's stuff I'd do differently if we did it again.

lines.text: You told Okafor. About Dez's slides. That's not nothing, doing that when you knew he'd be mad.
He's still kind of weird with me. But it got fixed. That's what mattered.
For the record — I was terrified you'd told him and it would blow up the whole group. It didn't. So. Thank you.
I mean, you didn't rat anyone out. You just... made us actually fix it. Together.
Two AM, rewriting Dez's slides together. Never doing that again. Probably.
It worked, though. That's the annoying part. The middle way actually worked.
You didn't say much, most of the project. Kind of surprised you're saying all this now.
Yeah. I know. I'm working on it.
It's a start.
You told Okafor, you covered for Dez in front of the whole panel — and you're still saying you'd do it differently?
There's always something. I could've said something to Dez sooner. Before it got to the plagiarism part.
Nobody's perfect at this. You were pretty close, though.
I mean, you kept trying to find the middle of everything. That's basically the whole story.
Yeah. Didn't always land, though.
It landed enough.
...Yeah. I mean, there were a couple points where it felt like nobody wanted to say anything. Including you.
I know. I noticed that too. Kind of late.
At least you're noticing now.
...Yeah. Sure.
He starts to walk off, then stops, like he almost said something else — something about how the two of them actually got Dez to redo it right.
He wanted to talk about it. The real part. And I changed the subject anyway.
Cool. Yeah. Grade's good.
We did the hard part right, in the middle of all that. Feels weird not saying that out loud.
...Yeah. Guess it worked out.
He walks off before she can find anything else to say.
It worked out. So why does it feel like something's missing.

voiceNotes: Charlie deflects with humor and hedges ('I mean, maybe it's just me') right up until she has to actually commit to a choice — her choice-point lines are noticeably more direct than her ambient dialogue, which is intentional; the moments she can't soften are the moments that matter. Dez's confidence is loudest right before he's cornered — his lines get shorter and less punctuated ('...Yeah. I know.') the closer he gets to an honest admission. Priya's speech speeds up under stress (run-on sentences, precise vocab like 'that's not an insignificant risk' energy) and then she catches herself and clips it short. Mo almost never initiates dialogue with full sentences — his lines trail off or hedge ('I don't really get this part' instead of asking for help) until he's earned enough trust to make a joke, at which point his humor is drier and more confident than his silence suggested. Mr. Okafor never over-explains; his approval is always understated ('Good.') and his questions do the work his statements could've done. NARRATOR lines are used sparingly, only to carry physical/scene information that can't be shown through dialogue, keeping focus on voice.

alternatives: We think it might be about timing, but we couldn't find a source solid enough to say that for sure, so we didn't want to just guess out loud.
Honestly, we ran out of time to nail that part down completely, and I'd rather say that than make something up.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps the honest-answer moment specific and age-appropriate without requiring the writer to invent believable in-universe science content that might date the script.

alternatives: Yeah, well. Try telling the smart group you're not smart enough and see how that goes.
Yeah. It's easier to fake it than to say I didn't get it as fast as everybody else.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Dez's insecurity needs to land as a genuine crack in his bravado, not a speech — these alternatives keep it clipped and defensive rather than eloquent, which fits his established pattern of getting 'short and clipped' when cornered.

alternatives: So, we're good, right? Grade came back fine.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Alternative phrasing for the deflection in the uneasy ending, in case 'that's the main thing, right?' reads too on-the-nose about the theme rather than sounding like natural subject-changing.
